_comment: Curation config: title patterns (lowercase substring on normalized title) + optional date_min/date_max assign raw hits to tracked cases. order_notes maps order dates to gists/significance. All facts below verified against fetched order text unless marked (verify).

tracked_cases.title: S. Rajaseekaran v. Union of India
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: The master road-safety PIL, filed by orthopaedic surgeon Dr. S. Rajaseekaran. Created the SC Committee on Road Safety (22 Apr 2014), forced state road-safety policies and lead agencies (30 Nov 2017), produced the Jan 2024 hit-and-run compensation directions, and is now driving vehicle-tracking and speed-governor enforcement.
tracked_cases.latest_development: 13 May 2026: Bench of Pardiwala & Viswanathan JJ (amicus Gaurav Agrawal) took up IAs on Vehicle Location Tracking Devices and emergency buttons in public service vehicles (Rule 125H CMVR), speed governors, the National Road Safety Board, and a challenge to UP legislation abating MV Act prosecutions.

2014-04-22.gist: Constituted the Supreme Court Committee on Road Safety (Justice K.S. Radhakrishnan) with directions to states on implementation reporting.

2017-11-30.gist: Wide-ranging directions: every state to have a road safety policy, council, lead agency, road safety fund, action plan, engineering review of designs, trauma-care measures and protocols.

2024-01-12.gist: Directions on the hit-and-run compensation scheme under S.161 MV Act — awareness drives, police obligation to inform victims, GIC reporting and monitoring of claims.

2026-05-13.gist: Examined VLTD/emergency-button enforcement in public service vehicles, speed-limiting devices, National Road Safety Board functioning, and UP's abatement of MV Act prosecutions.

2025-05-14.gist: Pedestrian-safety order (Oka & Bhuyan JJ) on IA 50798/2025: footpaths in proper condition — accessible to persons with disabilities and free of encroachments — held part of Article 21, following Olga Tellis; nationwide pedestrian-infrastructure norms taken up. Same order: a final six months to constitute the National Road Safety Board under S.215B.

tracked_cases.title: In Re: Phalodi Accident (Suo Motu)
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Registered suo motu after twin crashes killed 34 people in Nov 2025 — 15 near Phalodi (Rajasthan, Bharatmala expressway) and 19 near Chevella (Telangana). Now the most active systemic matter: Article 142 interim directions to MoRTH, NHAI and all states on expressway safety, blackspots and illegal roadside obstructions. NH carry ~2% of road length but ~30% of fatalities.
tracked_cases.latest_development: 13 Apr 2026 (2026 INSC 388, Maheshwari & Chandurkar JJ): interim directions (a)–(m) under Article 142 — national policy framework with deadlines, removal of illegal parking/dhabas, blackspot rectification, agencies jointly liable; MoRTH to file district-wise compliance in 75 days and route the Justice Sapre Road Safety Committee's recommendations to the Court. Listed for compliance after two months.

2025-11-10.gist: Suo motu cognizance of the Phalodi (Rajasthan) and Chevella (Telangana) crashes of 2–3 Nov 2025; notice to Rajasthan and central highway authorities.

2026-04-13.gist: Reportable Article 142 interim directions (a)–(m): national expressway-safety policy framework, blackspot and illegal-obstruction removal, joint agency liability, MoRTH compliance report in 75 days, Sapre Committee recommendations to be placed before the Court.

tracked_cases.title: SaveLIFE Foundation v. Union of India (Good Samaritan)
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: The Good Samaritan case: 30 Mar 2016 judgment gave MoRTH's 2015 Good Samaritan guidelines the force of law, protecting bystanders who help crash victims — later codified as S.134A MV Act.
tracked_cases.latest_development: Disposed 30 Mar 2016; guidelines later codified as S.134A MV Act (2019 amendment).

2016-03-30.gist: Judgment: MoRTH's Good Samaritan guidelines and SOPs given the force of law under Article 141/142, binding on all states until legislation.

tracked_cases.title: SaveLIFE Foundation v. Union of India (Emergency Care)
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: SaveLIFE's second-generation petition: integrate all emergency helplines into 112, Good Samaritan grievance redressal in every state, and full operationalization of cashless treatment. The May 2026 order turned PM RAHAT rollout into a court-monitored obligation.
tracked_cases.latest_development: 26 May 2026 (reportable, Maheshwari J): all states/UTs directed to fully operationalize PM RAHAT within 8 weeks–3 months (hospital designation, SHA on TMS, police on eDAR, DC sub-agency accounts), integrate helplines with 112, run multilingual mass-media campaigns on 112/S.134A/cashless treatment, and file action-taken reports. Non-implementation declared a violation of the MV Act. Listed after four months.

2026-05-26.gist: Reportable order: PM RAHAT operationalization within 8 weeks–3 months made a court-monitored obligation on all states/UTs; 112 helpline integration; Good Samaritan grievance systems; mass-media campaigns; ATRs to the Registry; listed after 4 months.

tracked_cases.title: Gohar Mohammed v. Uttar Pradesh SRTC
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: 15 Dec 2022 judgment (Nazeer & Maheshwari JJ) issued nationwide directions on the Detailed Accident Report workflow — police/insurer timelines, MACT registration, coordination portal — reshaping how compensation claims begin. Compliance is monitored through MA 825/2023.
tracked_cases.latest_development: 5 May 2026: brief order in MA 825/2023 — matter listed for August 2026; court-held fixed deposit renewed.

2022-12-15.gist: Judgment with nationwide DAR directions: police to register and forward Detailed Accident Reports to MACTs within fixed timelines, insurers to respond on liability, states to coordinate through a web portal.

tracked_cases.title: State of Tamil Nadu v. K. Balu
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Banned liquor vends within 500m of national and state highways (Dec 2016), relying on National Road Safety Council advice; scope clarified for municipal areas in 2017. The landmark drunk-driving-prevention order.
tracked_cases.latest_development: Disposed; 31 Mar 2017 clarification modified the ban's application.

2016-12-15.gist: Liquor vends prohibited within 500m of national/state highways; licences not to be renewed after 31 Mar 2017; signage advertising liquor on highways banned.

2017-03-31.gist: Clarification: distance reduced for smaller municipal areas; implementation modalities settled.

tracked_cases.title: Bajaj Allianz Gen. Insurance v. Rambha Devi (Constitution Bench)
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Five-judge bench (6 Nov 2024) settled whether an LMV licence covers transport vehicles under 7,500 kg — upholding Mukund Dewangan — while recording road-safety concerns about licensing for MoRTH to address through amendments.

2024-11-06.gist: Constitution Bench: LMV licence holders may drive transport vehicles below 7,500 kg GVW; licensing-regime concerns flagged for MoRTH's pending MV Act amendments.

tracked_cases.title: Gyan Prakash v. Union of India
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: National Highway safety PIL built on the Control of National Highways (Land and Traffic) Act 2002 and Highway Administration Rules 2004 — pressing for functioning highway administrations. The Justice Sapre Road Safety Committee reports to the Court through this matter.
tracked_cases.latest_development: 21 May 2025 (2025 INSC 753, Oka J): reportable order on highway safety under the 2002 Act; MoRTH directed to file details through the amicus curiae.

2025-05-21.gist: Reportable order (Oka J) on NH safety under the 2002 Act/2004 Rules — highway administrations, enforcement machinery; MoRTH filings directed via amicus.

tracked_cases.title: General Insurance Council v. State of A.P.
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Petition to enforce the Rajaseekaran order of 22 Apr 2014 across states — an example of how the master PIL's directions were operationalised through satellite litigation.

tracked_cases.title: Common Cause v. Union of India
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Early PIL on deficient road infrastructure and traffic-control devices. Dismissed (11 Apr 2008, Katju J) holding courts cannot direct legislation — the doctrinal gap that Rajaseekaran later filled through continuing mandamus.

2008-04-11.gist: PIL seeking a Road Traffic Safety Act dismissed: courts cannot direct Parliament to legislate; judgment doubles as a critique of PIL overreach.

tracked_cases.title: Avishek Goenka v. Union of India
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Banned black films / dark tinted glass on vehicles beyond prescribed visual light transmission (Apr 2012) on road-safety and crime-prevention grounds.

2012-04-27.gist: Black films and tints beyond prescribed VLT banned on all vehicles; police empowered to remove them on the spot.

tracked_cases.title: Ajay Canu v. Union of India
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Upheld compulsory helmet rules (1988) against constitutional challenge — the foundation for helmet mandates nationwide.

1988-08-29.gist: Compulsory helmet requirement upheld as a valid road-safety measure, not an unconstitutional restriction on personal liberty.

tracked_cases.title: State of Haryana v. Arrive Safe Society of Chandigarh
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Precursor to K. Balu: SC refused to disturb the P&H High Court's interim orders against Haryana's policy of licensing liquor shops along National Highways, noting the MoRTH Road Safety Cell circular.

2014-03-10.gist: SLP dismissed: Haryana's highway liquor-licence policy left to High Court scrutiny; MoRTH circular noted approvingly.

tracked_cases.title: Pt. Parmanand Katara v. Union of India
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: The foundation of post-crash care law in India: every doctor, government or private, must provide immediate emergency treatment to accident victims without waiting for police formalities. Every later Good Samaritan and cashless-treatment direction builds on this.
tracked_cases.latest_development: Disposed 28 Aug 1989; the duty-to-treat principle now underpins S.134A MV Act and PM RAHAT.

1989-08-28.gist: Judgment: the right to life obliges doctors to treat road-accident victims immediately; procedural formalities (police clearance, medico-legal registration) cannot delay emergency care.

tracked_cases.title: Paramjit Bhasin v. Union of India
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: The overloading case: struck at state notifications that let overloaded commercial vehicles ply on payment, holding axle-load limits under the MV Act must be enforced, not monetised — overloading being a major crash and road-damage cause.

2005-11-09.gist: Judgment on Art. 32: state notifications permitting overloaded vehicles to operate on compounding payments held impermissible; load limits under the MV Act to be strictly enforced.

match: jai prakash vs m/s national insurance
tracked_cases.title: Jai Prakash v. National Insurance Co.
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: The pre-Gohar Mohammed reform vehicle: a reportable three-judge order (17 Dec 2009) confronting four systemic MACT failures — uncompensated victims of hit-and-run and uninsured vehicles, delayed claim registration, and police-tribunal coordination — with follow-up orders into 2018.
tracked_cases.latest_development: Disposed; monitoring orders ran through Feb 2018. Its reporting mechanism was later expanded by Gohar Mohammed (2022).

2009-12-17.gist: Reportable order (Raveendran, Radhakrishnan, Sharma JJ) addressing four recurring MACT problems, including the ~20% of victims left uncompensated by hit-and-run and uninsured vehicles, and directing procedural fixes.

tracked_cases.title: Shishupal @ Shish Ram v. Surjeet
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: The homemaker-valuation judgment: fixed ₹30,000/month as the floor value of a homemaker's work — a new 'loss of domestic care' head rising 10% cumulatively every three years — recast homemakers as 'Nation Builders', and issued nationwide directions to High Courts and tribunals on clearing decades-old MACT appeals. The family here had waited 25 years.
tracked_cases.latest_development: Decided 11 Jun 2026 (Karol & Kotiswar Singh JJ); judgment circulated to the Registrars General of all High Courts for compliance.

2026-06-11.gist: Reportable judgment: composite ₹30,000/month 'loss of domestic care' head created (stand-in minimum income for non-earning homemakers; additional to proved income otherwise); chronological oldest-first listing of MACT appeals in all High Courts; tribunals nudged to S.169 summary procedure; Pranay Sethi heads with 10%/3-year enhancement strictly enforced.

tracked_cases.title: Re: Fundamental Right to Walk and Footpath (Maniyar Iliyaz v. P. Ayyappan)
tracked_cases.case_number: C.A. 4665-4666/2025, re-numbered as Art. 32 petition · 2026 INSC 647
tracked_cases.why_it_matters: Born from a five-year-old killed walking to school on a road with no footpath or crossing: the Court declared the right to walk a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(d), placed a correlative duty on urban development authorities, municipalities and panchayats to demarcate, build and maintain footpaths, and made breach actionable for restitution and compensation independent of the MV Act. The Court then converted the disposed appeal into a continuing Article 32 matter, impleading MoHUA, MoRD and MoRTH.
tracked_cases.latest_development: 19 Jun 2026 (Narasimha & Chandurkar JJ): judgment delivered; Registry directed to re-register the case as 'Re: Fundamental Right to Walk and Footpath' with the Union impleaded in person — the pedestrian-infrastructure counterpart to Phalodi.

2026-06-19.gist: Reportable judgment: the right to walk on demarcated footpaths recognised as a facet of Art. 19(1)(d); duty bearers named (urban development authorities, municipal bodies, panchayats); violations remediable through constitutional and legal claims independent of the MV Act; case converted into a continuing Article 32 petition with amicus and ASG assistance.

notable_judgments.title: Sarla Verma v. Delhi Transport Corporation
notable_judgments.gist: Standardised the multiplier method for motor accident compensation — the arithmetic every MACT in India now uses.

notable_judgments.title: National Insurance Co. v. Pranay Sethi (Constitution Bench)
notable_judgments.gist: Settled 'future prospects' additions and conventional heads for just compensation, resolving conflicting benches.

notable_judgments.title: State of Tamil Nadu v. K. Balu
notable_judgments.gist: Liquor vends banned within 500m of highways — drunk-driving prevention as a constitutional road-safety obligation.

notable_judgments.title: Bajaj Allianz v. Rambha Devi (Constitution Bench)
notable_judgments.gist: LMV licence holders may drive transport vehicles under 7,500 kg; licensing-reform concerns flagged to MoRTH.

notable_judgments.title: Regional Transport Authority v. Shaju
notable_judgments.gist: Upheld Kerala rule letting RTAs refuse replacement of a permit vehicle with an older one (S.83 MV Act) — vehicle age and road-worthiness as valid safety criteria.

notable_judgments.title: Alister Anthony Pareira v. State of Maharashtra
notable_judgments.gist: Upheld conviction and custodial sentence in the Mumbai drunk-driving pavement deaths case — deterrence in drunk-driving sentencing.

notable_judgments.title: Reshma Kumari v. Madan Mohan
notable_judgments.gist: Three-judge bench affirming Sarla Verma's multiplier approach — bound the compensation method ahead of Pranay Sethi.

notable_judgments.title: Gohar Mohammed v. UP SRTC
notable_judgments.gist: Nationwide Detailed Accident Report directions — police and insurer timelines that begin every compensation claim (also a tracked case).

notable_judgments.title: G.M., Kerala SRTC v. Susamma Thomas
notable_judgments.gist: Established the multiplier method as the governing approach to computing fatal-accident compensation.

notable_judgments.title: U.P. SRTC v. Trilok Chandra
notable_judgments.gist: Recalibrated the multiplier method and capped multipliers — the bridge between Susamma Thomas and Sarla Verma.

notable_judgments.title: Manjusri Raha v. B.L. Gupta
notable_judgments.gist: Early three-judge call for realistic, non-conservative compensation in fatal road accidents.

notable_judgments.title: Nagappa v. Gurudayal Singh
notable_judgments.gist: Tribunals may award compensation beyond the amount claimed — just compensation is not capped by the pleading.

notable_judgments.title: National Insurance Co. v. Swaran Singh (3-judge)
notable_judgments.gist: Licence-defect defences narrowed: the insurer must prove a wilful, fundamental breach to escape third-party liability; pay-and-recover preserved.

notable_judgments.title: Sohan Lal Passi v. P. Sesh Reddy
notable_judgments.gist: An insurer escapes liability only for the insured's wilful breach of licence conditions — protecting third-party claimants.

notable_judgments.title: Shivaji Dayanu Patil v. Vatschala Uttam More
notable_judgments.gist: 'Arising out of the use of a motor vehicle' read expansively — a tanker explosion hours after the collision remained compensable.

notable_judgments.title: Magma General Insurance v. Nanu Ram
notable_judgments.gist: Recognised loss of consortium in its spousal, parental and filial forms as distinct heads of compensation.

notable_judgments.title: United India Insurance v. Satinder Kaur
notable_judgments.gist: Consolidated the post-Pranay Sethi law on just compensation — income assessment, future prospects and conventional heads.

notable_judgments.title: Kirti v. Oriental Insurance
notable_judgments.gist: Fixed notional income for homemakers and extended future prospects to notional-income cases — recognising unpaid domestic work.

notable_judgments.title: Kajal v. Jagdish Chand
notable_judgments.gist: A comprehensive framework for compensating a child left with 100% disability — attendant care, future treatment, interest.

notable_judgments.title: Kurvan Ansari v. Shyam Kishore Murmu
notable_judgments.gist: Raised the notional-income basis for deceased children, criticising states' failure to update compensation schedules.

notable_judgments.title: Dalbir Singh v. State of Haryana
notable_judgments.gist: Sentencing in S.304A road-death cases: leniency towards rash drivers undermines deterrence — the custodial norm affirmed.

notable_judgments.title: State of Punjab v. Saurabh Bakshi
notable_judgments.gist: Deterrent sentencing in rash-driving deaths reiterated, with a call to the legislature to revisit inadequate penalties.

notable_judgments.title: State v. Sanjeev Nanda (BMW hit-and-run)
notable_judgments.gist: Drunk-driving death treated as culpable homicide (S.304 Part II) — a marker for the accountability of intoxicated drivers.

notable_judgments.title: Mangla Ram v. Oriental Insurance
notable_judgments.gist: MACT claims are decided on preponderance of probability — strict criminal-trial proof standards do not apply.

notable_judgments.title: Mukund Dewangan v. Oriental Insurance (3-judge)
notable_judgments.gist: LMV licence holders may drive transport vehicles under 7,500 kg — later affirmed by the Rambha Devi Constitution Bench.

notable_judgments.title: Shishupal @ Shish Ram v. Surjeet
notable_judgments.gist: Created the 'loss of domestic care' head: ₹30,000/month — rising 10% every three years — as the minimum value of a homemaker's work in MACT cases; a stand-in income for non-earning homemakers, additional to proved income for working women.

notable_judgments.title: Baby Sakshi Greola v. Manzoor Ahmad Simon
notable_judgments.gist: A seven-year-old struck by a speeding car on a zebra crossing: the Court set aside the High Court's order and enhanced the MACT compensation, insurer to pay within eight weeks — pedestrian priority at crossings, vindicated in compensation law.

borderline_excluded.why: Transport aggregator regulation (bike taxis) — road safety cited but the core issue is platform licensing
Vehicular pollution matter (Delhi), not crash safety — occasional adjacent orders (colour-coded stickers)
Disposed citing Rajaseekaran as already covering the field
cases: Anti Corruption Council of India v. MoRTH (2021)

borderline_excluded.why: Driving-licence qualification notifications (Karnataka) — watch; may merit tracking if systemic directions issue
cases: S.G. Jamaludheen v. State of Karnataka (2025)

borderline_excluded.why: MACT pendency grievance (Orissa) — individual relief but symptomatic; watch
cases: Minati Dash v. Registrar, Orissa HC (2026)

borderline_excluded.why: Accessibility-rights matter (persons with disabilities) whose directions cover footpaths and pedestrian infrastructure — overlaps the Rajaseekaran pedestrian IA; promote if road-specific directions issue
cases: Rajive Raturi v. Union of India (2017 order; 2024 judgment)

borderline_excluded.why: Footpath-encroachment and street-vending jurisprudence (livelihood/municipal law, not crash safety) — the backdrop to pedestrian-infrastructure orders
cases: Olga Tellis (1985) · Sodan Singh (1989) · Gainda Ram · Maharashtra Ekta Hawkers Union · Footpath Vikreta Kalyan Samiti (ongoing)
